description: OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) — used by smart-TV, IoT, CLI tools. Phishing surface: attacker initiates device flow on victim's account, sends victim the user_code via social engineering (email, deep link), gets victim to approve at the verification URI.
craft_guidance: 1. IDENTIFY DEVICE FLOW: presence of /oauth/device/code endpoint, user_code field, verification_uri, OR app supports CLI/IoT clients.
2. REGISTER NEW DEVICE: POST to /device/code with client_id; receive device_code + user_code + verification_uri.
3. PHISH USER_CODE: short codes (XXX-YYY format) are easy to social-engineer. Test if the verification_uri pre-fills the code, making victim's job a single click.
4. POLL TIMING: how long is device_code valid? Can attacker race victim's approval?
5. SCOPE ESCALATION: request scope=admin or scope=offline_access at /device/code. If approval grants without warning, low-privacy UX = full ATO.
6. RATE LIMIT: poll /token endpoint as fast as possible (no rate limit on poll = brute-force user_code possible).
7. NO USER CONFIRM: some servers issue tokens before user clicks 'Approve'. Test by polling immediately.

device_code_phishing.description: Standard device-flow phishing — attacker initiates flow, victim approves attacker's session.

probes.description: Initiate device flow with admin scope. If granted post-approval, scope-creep phishing works.
Poll /token immediately without user approval. Some misconfigured servers grant token if device_code is valid (no authorization_pending check).

user_code_brute_force.description: User code is short (e.g. 6-8 chars). If poll endpoint has no rate limit, attacker brute-forces a victim's pending user_code.

probes.description: Submit random user_code to /device/verify endpoint at high rate. If no rate limit + no CAPTCHA, code is enumerable.
